data.title: From Epicenter To Aftermath: Preparing For The Economic Impact Of Trump's Tariffs
data.description:
On the morning of December 26, 2004, a massive undersea earthquake - one of the strongest ever recorded - struck off the coast of Sumatra, Indonesia. In the immediate aftermath, there was little sense of the devastation to come. The true destruction unfolded hours later, as tsunami waves surged across the Indian Ocean, striking coastal communities with little warning and overwhelming force. What began as a sudden seismic event quickly evolved into one of the deadliest natural disasters in history, with its full impact only understood as the waves rippled outward.

In a similar way, President Trump’s announcement on April 2nd of the largest tariff increases in over a century feels like an economic earthquake of a magnitude not seen since COVID. The initial shock has been delivered, but the true consequences are still forming beneath the surface. Just as the tsunami’s waves took time to reach distant shores, the effects of these sweeping tariffs will ripple through supply chains, consumer prices, and global markets in the weeks and months ahead - with outcomes that are, at this moment, largely unknown.

Trump’s Tariffs - Economic Chaos or Grand Strategy?

President Trump’s goal is to bring manufacturing back to the United States - an industry that was hollowed-out after China joined the World Trade Organization (WTO) in 2001. Within a decade of China’s entry, the U.S. lost about one-third of its manufacturing workforce, and it has never fully recovered. For nearly half a century, the U.S. maintained between 16 and 20 million manufacturing jobs, but since 2001, that number has steadily declined, as shown in the figure below.

(Source: Bloomberg, Financial Sense Wealth Management)

A recent presentation by Michael Cembalest, Chairman of Market and Investment Strategy at J.P. Morgan, sheds light on what Trump is trying to fix ("Chaos vs Grand Design: Trump 2.0 for Investors"). One of his charts shows U.S. consumer spending, Gross Domestic Product (GDP), and industrial production (a key measure of manufacturing output) over time. For more than 70 years, these three indicators moved in lockstep. However, after China’s WTO entry, U.S. manufacturing diverged - falling behind even as consumer spending and GDP continued to rise.

You can see the evidence in cities like Detroit and other once-thriving manufacturing hubs, now marked by decay and economic decline.

While American consumers did benefit from lower prices for goods made in China, the cost was high; the decline in manufacturing jobs coincided with a surge in opioid addictions, rising suicide rates, and falling manufacturing wages relative to the service sector.

We also saw the consequences during the COVID-19 pandemic, when the U.S. was heavily reliant on foreign countries like China and India for medicines and personal protective equipment. Simply put, the U.S. no longer produces many essential goods at home.

To illustrate how fragile our manufacturing capacity has become, Mr. Cembalest points to data showing that it may now take anywhere from 2 to 18 years to replace critical U.S. munitions transferred to Ukraine.

This raises a serious question: how could the U.S. respond to a strategic threat, such as a potential Chinese invasion of Taiwan, if it takes years to rebuild critical military supplies?

Measuring the Damage

Following President Trump’s tariff announcement, Wall Street economists are quickly raising their recession forecasts. They now expect negative real GDP growth for the next two quarters - and possibly for the first quarter as well, given the surge in imports as businesses rushed to beat the tariffs.

Just like the full impact of the 2004 earthquake wasn’t immediately clear, it’s difficult today to fully predict the economic consequences ahead. Key questions remain:

  • How much will businesses pull back on capital spending?
  • How will that impact overall business income?
  • Will companies freeze hiring?
  • What will be the level of retaliation from China or others?

Returning to the earthquake analogy, the longer Trump’s "tariff earthquake" continues, the greater the damage could be. Just as a prolonged earthquake releases more energy and stresses structures until they collapse, extended tariffs strain global trade and supply chains.

The United States is the world’s largest economy, and the sudden imposition of tariffs at this scale could cause the most severe disruption to global trade since COVID. That’s why quickly negotiating new trade deals - and lowering tariff rates - is critical.

If not, we risk eroding the few remaining supports holding up the U.S. economy and tipping into a recession. DOGE (the Department of Government Efficiency) is attempting to identify areas of fraud, wasteful spending, and reverse the high levels of recessionary spending seen under the Biden administration that kept the economy afloat. Meanwhile, high-end consumers - who helped sustain overall retail spending - are likely to pull back after recent stock market losses.

Typically, a recession drives the budget deficit up by 4-5% of GDP. With the deficit already running at 7% of GDP, a downturn could push it to 11-12% - higher than the 10% seen during the 2007-2009 Financial Crisis, and second only to the 18% during the COVID-induced recession.

Investors Stop Panicking When Policymakers Start Panicking

Looking back over the last quarter century of stock market volatility reveals that investor panics are often followed by policymaker panics, whose stimulus halts the market’s decline and sparks a renewed bull market. This pattern - where a market decline and a spike in volatility are countered by stimulus - is illustrated below, with the S&P 500 at top and the Volatility Index (VIX) at bottom. The largest spikes in the VIX (2008/2020) resulted in the largest fiscal and monetary stimulus responses, while milder panics led to more measured stimulus.

(Source: Financial Sense Wealth Management, Bloomberg. Note: Indices are unmanaged and cannot be invested into directly.)

On Monday, April 7th, the VIX spiked to 60 - a level rarely seen - and markets began to stabilize after an extremely volatile session. While VIX levels this high are often associated with major buying opportunities, they usually require a catalyst: a clear policy response. At present, we have neither - which is why holding cash remains crucial.

Federal Reserve Chairman Jerome Powell gave a speech on Friday that offered little sense of urgency, even as markets increasingly priced in rate cuts. On April 1st, markets expected three rate cuts this year; by April 7th, expectations rose to nearly four.

Without a monetary response, the Trump administration remains the market’s best hope. A potential catalyst would be Trump suspending the tariffs for 90 days to allow negotiations and ease uncertainty. However, administration comments have downplayed that possibility, with the tariff deadline of April 9th still in place. There have been leaks suggesting Trump may offer tax cuts to exporters hurt by retaliatory tariffs, but nothing definitive yet.

Outlook and Strategy

Markets are extremely oversold and primed for a relief rally. However, the strength and duration of any rally will depend on a policy shift from either the Fed or the Trump administration. Without such a pivot, the upside for stocks appears limited - which is why we remain cautious.

Goldman Sachs’ economics team is likely to make a U.S. recession its baseline forecast if tariffs go into effect on April 9th without a policy response. U.S. financial markets would likely continue to struggle.

Internationally, however, some countries are already preparing stimulus to soften the blow. Spain unveiled a $15.7 billion plan to protect industries from tariffs, and China’s policymakers are considering accelerating stimulus efforts. This could allow foreign markets to outperform U.S. markets, especially given stronger fiscal support abroad.

Capital tends to flow where it’s best treated. We may be witnessing a major shift in global capital flows, first sparked by the Biden administration’s weaponization of the U.S. dollar and SWIFT system during the 2022 Russia-Ukraine conflict, and now accelerated by Trump's tariff policy.

The US Dollar Index (DXY) peaked in 2022, and since then, the S&P 500 has made little progress relative to foreign stocks. More importantly, the outperformance of U.S. stocks relative to foreign stocks is on the verge of breaking a trend that has been in place since coming out of the Great Financial Crisis of 2007-2009 (see blue trend line below).

Looking at a long-term chart of the relative performance of the S&P 500 relative to foreign stocks shows that U.S. stocks tend to outperform (rising line in top panel) when the Dollar Index rises and tend to underperform when the Dollar Index falls (falling line in top panel). U.S. stocks greatly outperformed in the 1990s during the technology bubble and from 2009-2022, as shown by the green shaded regions. The last great period of underperformance came in the 2000s as the technology bubble burst and the Dollar Index plummeted as the U.S. budget deficit blew out from funding the post 9-11 terrorist attacks.

(Source: Financial Sense Wealth Management, Bloomberg. Note: Indices are unmanaged and cannot be invested into directly.)

As mentioned previously, a U.S. recession could see the budget deficit blow out to double-digits, which would likely cause Treasury debt issuance to surge and put downward pressure on the dollar and likely lead to further underperformance of the U.S. stock market relative to foreign stocks. Just as the Biden administration’s weaponization of the dollar and SWIFT payment system led to a peak in the dollar and a shift towards central banks gravitating to gold, the Trump tariff policy may further shift global capital out of U.S. financial assets.

This is why our firm is looking to use the current global sell-off as an opportunity to scoop in and increase our exposure to foreign stock markets where there is more fiscal and monetary stimulus to support their financial markets. This new focus in the portfolios would complement our existing anti-dollar exposure we already have with our precious metals holdings.

While foreign markets look increasingly attractive, there is also tremendous value emerging in the U.S. market.

As of Friday’s close:

  • Over 10% of S&P 500 stocks had dividend yields above 5%.
  • Over 20% had yields above 4%.
  • More than 20% of S&P 500 companies announced share buybacks that would retire over 10% of their outstanding shares - providing strong support for share prices.

Dividend and buyback yields at these levels haven’t been seen since the COVID panic of 2020 or the bottom of the 2007-2009 Financial Crisis.

In short, while we are increasing our exposure to foreign markets, we are also excited about deep value opportunities at home. We believe this balanced approach positions us well to navigate the challenging but opportunity-rich environment ahead. In times like these, staying flexible is critical. We are actively monitoring policy developments and adjusting portfolios to capture opportunities - both abroad and here at home - while maintaining a cautious, risk-managed approach. If you have any questions regarding your portfolio or our investment strategy, please do not hesitate to reach out to your wealth manager.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Nvidia: Get Out While You Still Can
data.description:
It’s been slightly over a week since President Trump announced his trade policy that unleashed the global trade war. At this point, it’s safe to say that such a sudden increase in tariffs that was announced last week will likely disrupt the global economy and result in a major recession in the foreseeable future. In the next few weeks, we will likely witness major downward revenue and earnings revisions from the Wall Street community as companies brace for turmoil in this brave new world.

For NVIDIA Corporation (NASDAQ:NVDA), the trade war has come at one of the worst times possible. After the initial uptick in demand for its GPUs that began in 2023 and helped the company generate triple-digit returns while its stock soared to new highs, the company recently started to face significant challenges. In our latest article, we already highlighted some of those challenges like the inability to increase its market share in China due to export restrictions along with the normalization of the growth rate, which indicated that the aggressive growth rate is likely to become a thing of the past. Nvidia’s stock has declined by around 30% since we published that article in late 2024, and we believe that the worst is not over yet.

The company continues to face a rising number of major risks, its stock remains overvalued even after the recent fall, and considering the latest macro developments we believe that it’s better to dump Nvidia’s shares since the downside remains significant.

Growth Is Under Threat

Nvidia ended 2024 on a high note as its revenues soared, and the street largely remained bullish about its growth prospects. But the sentiment nevertheless was slowly changing.

Even before the Q4 results were announced, we witnessed the rise of Chinese LLMs, which apparently are much more efficient in comparison to its Western peers as they appear to show similar results as the competition at a fraction of the cost. This made the market raise the question of whether hyperscalers and other companies even need a significant amount of Nvidia’s GPUs to run their models. Then recently we received mixed signals about Nvidia’s latest GB300 servers, which apparently are not in high demand. Finally, last week Bloomberg reported that Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) has paused a number of its data center projects, which might indicate that there’s an overcapacity of AI data centers. If that’s the case, then there’s a risk that Nvidia might underperform in the upcoming quarters due to the potential lower demand for its AI accelerators.

In addition to that, Nvidia’s major clients are also slowly becoming the company’s direct competitors. Businesses like Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN), OpenAI, Google (GOOG, GOOGL), and Meta Platforms, Inc. (META) are preparing to release their custom-made chips that can decrease their dependency on Nvidia and undermine the latter’s dominance over the AI accelerator market. While this is not going to happen overnight, Nvidia’s sales nevertheless could take a hit over time if its major clients release their own chips and no longer rely as much on its GPUs as before.

Although the rise of competitors and the potential decrease in demand are certainly a big issue for Nvidia, the escalation of the global trade war is becoming an even greater problem for the company. Even last year Nvidia had problems with scaling its operations in China, as its data center sales there continue to be below the pre-export control levels to this day. The strengthening of export restrictions by the United States last month will likely make it even harder for Nvidia to expand its operations in the Chinese market in the foreseeable future.

At the same time, the announcement of new tariffs by the United States and the subsequent retaliation by China along with the potential retaliation by the European Union create a possibility of a major supply chain disruption that will likely hinder Nvidia’s growth. If the Trump administration also imposes tariffs on chipmakers, then we don’t see a scenario where Nvidia can continue to generate aggressive returns and significantly exceed its expectations anytime soon.

The Real Value of Nvidia

The deterioration of the macro environment is a major negative development for Nvidia, as the company’s growth rate along with its overall performance will likely suffer in the upcoming quarters. In late 2024, before the escalation of the global trade war, we believed that Nvidia was already overvalued. It was trading at $132 per share when we published our article, while our valuation model was showing that the business’s intrinsic value was $91.20 per share at that time. Although the company now trades around that level, we believe that its intrinsic value is even lower due to the latest macro and other developments described earlier in this article. In our opinion, Nvidia’s shares have not reached the bottom yet and have more room to fall.

To prove that, we have updated our valuation model, which now includes numbers from the latest earnings period and newer assumptions that better reflect the current macro environment.

In the model, the perpetual growth rate remained at 3%. The model is made at a time when Nvidia is trading at $95.32 per share, the cash and total debt numbers have been taken from the recent earnings report, and the forecast period in the model covers the period of the next five years.

The discount rate is 11.16%. We figured it out by calculating Nvidia’s cost of debt, the after-tax cost of debt, and the cost of equity. The total debt number in the cost of debt calculation was taken from the latest earnings report, while all the other numbers such as the interest expense, income tax expense, and the income before tax are from the trailing twelve months. For the cost of equity calculation, we used a risk-free rate of 4.20%, a beta of 1.96, and a market return rate of 7.77%. The detailed calculations can be seen below.

The forecast table underwent several changes as well. For FY26, the current analysts’ expectations are that the sales growth rate will be around 56%, while we estimated a 51.3% Y/Y growth rate in our latest model from late 2024. However, we believe that there will be major downward sales revisions in the following months due to the escalation of the trade war, which will likely result in a decrease in the consensus growth rate. As such, we decreased our sales growth rate in FY26 to 50% Y/Y for now.

At the same time, there’s a decent chance that we will decrease it even lower once the Q1 results are announced later this spring and there’s a better understanding of how the current macro events will affect the company’s performance this year and beyond. For other years, we also decreased the annual sales growth rate from 20% which we assumed in the recent model, to 15% today, as risks continue to mount.

The EBIT margin in the model also decreased from 62% in the previous model to 60% today. There’s also a possibility that it will be decreased further considering the latest macro and geopolitical developments that threaten to disrupt Nvidia’s supply chain and decrease its profitability. The assumptions for other metrics were mostly unchanged in this model.

Under such assumptions, our valuation model shows that Nvidia’s enterprise value is $1.79 trillion, while its equity value is $1.83 trillion. That translated to an intrinsic value of $73.87 per share, which means that Nvidia is still overvalued by around 22%.

At the same time, there’s a possibility that the downside is even greater. However, we would need to wait for the Q1 earnings report to come out later in May to better understand how big of an impact the trade war will have on Nvidia’s performance and make further revisions to our assumptions.

Risks To Our Bearish Thesis

While the situation looks dire, there are still ways for Nvidia to avoid the worst-case scenario and undermine our bearish thesis. We already got signals that the Trump administration is willing to negotiate trade deals with other countries, which could ease the pressure on the global economy.

Nvidia could also look for ways to work with its partners to move the production of its advanced chips to the United States. Last year, Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM) and Nvidia already talked about producing chips in Arizona, while earlier this month there were reports that the Taiwanese manufacturer is willing to work with Intel Corporation (INTC) on a joint venture to operate chipmaking factories in the United States. If the production of Nvidia’s chips is moved to the United States over time, then the company would be able to somewhat mitigate the impact of the recently announced tariffs.

Final Thoughts

Currently, we believe that Nvidia’s stock still has plenty of downside despite the potential growth catalysts and is not an attractive investment. The increase in macro risks, the rise of competitors, and the potential deterioration of demand could result in the underperformance of the company’s business in the upcoming quarters. That is why still rate Nvidia as a SELL in the current environment.

data.title: Nvidia Just Got Too Cheap
data.description:
Nvidia Corporation’s (NASDAQ:NVDA) explosive growth has reshaped the AI era, but beneath the $39 billion quarters lies a more complex equation. From rising capital intensity and customer concentration to geopolitical friction, risks are mounting. Yet with valuation metrics near 10-year averages and forward growth intact, Nvidia may be offering investors a rare opportunity to buy a category leader at a relative discount.

Inside Nvidia’s AI Powerhouse: Growth Unmatched, Margins Under Pressure

Nvidia’s Data Center segment, now accounting for over 90% of overall revenue, is still the company’s undisputed growth powerhouse. In Q4 FY25 alone, this segment raked in $35.6 billion in revenue, more than doubling year-on-year and representing a sequential improvement of nearly 16%.

Driving this momentum is exploding demand for generative AI infrastructure, especially associated with Blackwell and H100 GPUs driving training and inference workloads in the hyperscalers, sovereign compute, and enterprise implementations. Blackwell’s scale and velocity, at $11 billion in revenue in its initial quarter, confirms Nvidia’s leadership in not only providing chips but entire AI factories.

However, the price of this scale is now increasingly evident. Gross margins, at still healthy levels of 73.5% (non-GAAP), are beginning to compress as Blackwell yield ramping and pricing aggression in inference-load configurations take their toll on profitability. Margins are expected to recover once the platform fully ramps, but the immediate indicator is high manufacturing costs and rising competitive bids, particularly from constructing custom silicon (CSPs) options. Nvidia's Data Center business is very capital-intensive, not just due to high-end silicon such as GB200 NV72 but also due to end-to-end investments in software overlays, systems architecture, as well as on-prem cloud services.

While Data Center dominates headlines, Nvidia’s established businesses, Gaming, Professional Visualization (Pro Viz), and Automotive, keep on churning out a valuable, if secondary, share of the company’s revenues. Gaming revenue declined 11% year-on-year to $2.54 billion in Q4 FY25. While this would indicate declining consumer demand, management ascribed the bulk of the softness to transitory supply limitations in channel inventories. With the introduction of the new RTX 50 series GPUs with FP4 inference and up to 3,400 AI TOPS, Nvidia is now focusing on AI-native gaming features that can perform local inference, supporting privacy-enhanced, low-latency capabilities such as generative NPCs as well as AI-driven rendering.

Professional Visualization jumped 10% year-over-year to $511 million, driven by growing use cases in enterprise generative AI, simulation, and design engineering. With Ansys, Cadence, and other tools increasingly optimized for Nvidia RTX products, enterprise demand is shifting away from static rendering of 3D objects toward dynamic, real-time digital twins and simulation environment training. These applications can potentially be linked into Nvidia’s broader Omniverse and Cosmos roadmaps as future monetization paths.

Automotive experienced the strongest growth among the secondary units, rising 103% year-on-year to $570 million in quarterly revenue. Growth is driven by the increasing adoption of DRIVE Thor platforms among major partners such as Toyota and Aurora, as well as centralization in EVs and AVs. However, impressive as it is in terms of percent growth, these businesses combined are under 8% of overall revenue. Their strategic value is in diversification, not size. The monetization of software-defined vehicles and industrial AI is in its beginning stages, depending on multi-faceted regulatory, infrastructural, and product-readiness challenges.

Profitability: Strength in Free Cash Flow, but Adjustment Counts

Nvidia's profitability profile remains distinct from that of its peers, courtesy of its strong operational performance and effective capital deployment. In Q4 FY25, the business generated $16.6 billion of cash flow from operations, up 45% year-on-year. Down sequentially 6%, courtesy of higher accounts receivables as well as inventory build related to the Blackwell ramp, this still reflects Nvidia’s massive cash-generating ability. On a top-line basis, the business generated $64.1 billion in operating cash flow for FY25, doubling the $28.1 billion generated in FY24. Free cash flow, net of capex, hit $60.7 billion for FY25, making Nvidia one of the most capital-intensive compounders in the S&P 500 (SP500).

Free cash flow is still healthy, but cash flow from operations sequentially fell 6% on account of higher inventories as well as accounts receivable tied to Blackwell production cycles. While the management called this out as a working capital movement that is cyclical in nature, it is noteworthy that even Nvidia is not immune to timing shocks. It also significantly stepped up its capital returns, repurchased $7.8 billion worth of shares, and ended the quarter at $43.2 billion in gross cash and $34.7 billion in net cash. This leaves Nvidia with plenty of flexibility in order to absorb R&D ramps, supply chain investments, as well as future M&A in networking, photonics, as well as AI software space.

However, the difference between GAAP and non-GAAP numbers has significantly widened due in large part to stock-based compensation. Nvidia reported $1.32 billion in SBC in Q4 FY25, or just under 3.4% of revenue. It is an inordinately high ratio for a megacap, and while aligning incentives during times of high innovation is useful, it undermines GAAP profitability and may rein in long-term per-share metrics unless controlled. As institutional investors increasingly compare total shareholder return with SBC dilution, this will be one worth tracking.

Nvidia’s Quiet Risk: When Big Customers Hold the Leverage

Customer concentration has been one of the most underrated dangers for Nvidia for a long time, and FY25 numbers validate that risk is still high. There were three direct customers who each generated over 11% of revenue for the year, while at least one indirect customer, a probable hyperscaler, gained over 10% via multiple resellers and OEMs. That sort of customer dependency is standard in semiconductor countries, but the size of Nvidia's exposure is what you would expect to see in an OEM, not in a platform diversifier.

On the one hand, they enable Nvidia to secure massive volume and long-term deals and co-design custom architectures for hyperscaler workloads. This has been the major competitive differentiator, facilitating deep collaboration with Google (GOOG)(GOOGL), Amazon (AMZN), and Microsoft (MSFT) AI stacks. On the other, this dependency introduces volatility in revenues linked with a few budget cycles, architectural refreshes, or Capex transitions. If any of these mega clients postpone orders, design products in-house, or divert workloads towards competing products, the impact on Nvidia’s numbers can be severe.

Regulatory and geopolitical factors exacerbate this problem. Nvidia's access to China, previously 20–25% of Data Center demand, has been restricted by increasing American export controls on high-performance chips. The Blackwell platform is under even stricter controls, with most of its SKUs having individual export licenses required for them. Though Nvidia has released "compliant" versions for these countries, their margin is thinner and volume unknown. Future export rule updates may constrain Nvidia's ability to service installed bases outside the country, cutting its long-term service revenue prospects and straining customer relationships in Tier 2 geos.

Trump's Tariff: How a 100% Threat to TSMC Could Reshape Nvidia’s AI Supply Chain

Nvidia's reliance on Taiwan chipmaker TSMC (TSM) has become increasingly politicized following the threat of up to 100% tariffs made by President Donald Trump, should the company not move towards localizing production within the country. While the Biden administration granted $6.6 billion in subsidies for TSMC's Arizona fab, the comments made under Trump indicate policy uncertainty for firms like Nvidia depending on TSMC for state-of-the-art GPU production, following the punitive tariff route. Though TSMC has pledged $100 billion in investments in America across five planned fabs, volume production, particularly for Nvidia's high-end Blackwell chips, is still several years away.

Tensions are compounded as well by the threat of 32% US tariffs on electronics coming in from Taiwan, with Taiwan's government actively pursuing tariff exemptions in exchange for increasing US trade and investment. For Nvidia, the jeopardy is stark and if Trump's threat of a 100% tariff is enacted and new Taiwan-oriented tariffs are instituted, it could send the cost of bringing in AI accelerators and GPU systems soaring. That would squeeze margins and could force Nvidia into tough pricing decisions.

Though Nvidia’s CEO Jensen Huang has indicated short-term adaptability with geographically diversifying production, the corporation’s significant reliance on TSMC’s advanced manufacturing and packaging technologies does not easily become replicable in-country. As political posturing ramps up, Nvidia’s formerly optimized global supply chain is now in unchartered terrain where policy whims, rather than technology, may dictate the cost and timing of AI progress.

Nevertheless, I don't see this risk materializing fully, since TSMC is too systemically important to both the global economy and U.S. national security and can backfire on America's economy severely.

Nvidia’s Reset: A Rare Discount on a Dominant Platform

NVDA today must be evaluated on its own terms, rather than on the standard semiconductor playbook. No longer just the cyclical chip seller it once was, Nvidia is now the operating system for AI infrastructure, providing not just the leading silicon on offer but the software, networking, and cloud layers that manage modern computing environments as well.

After a historic run-up, Nvidia shares recently experienced a healthy valuation reset at about $100 post-split along with the market drop. While the retreat is seen by some as symptomatic of market exhaustion, however, the stock’s level may be an attractive entry point for long-term investors.

While having maintained their still-strong growth path, Nvidia’s forward valuation has compressed significantly. Its EV/EBITDA multiple, once rising above 40x, is now in the high 20s. Nvidia's trailing Price-to-sales of 18x, which, despite its massive scale and dominance in AI infrastructure, is only slightly above its 10-year average, suggesting that the market is pricing in historical norms, not hyper-optimism, offering an attractive entry point relative to its long-term growth trajectory.

Bottom Line

Nvidia is performing at a record level, but the stakes are a record as well. Its model has become an AI-infrastructure utility with broad sector reach. However, concentration risk, margin uncertainty, and regulatory headwinds can create turbulence below the surface of such performance. Nvidia's valuation is based on a near-perfect runway, shareholders need to balance that with the rising complexity and external pressures.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: JPMorgan Chase: Possible Stagflation Would Crush Valuation
data.description:
JPMorgan Chase (NYSE:JPM) was described as the Nvidia (NVDA) of banking by Wells Fargo's analyst on the back of its ramping capital expenditure in its technology. JPM allocated $17 billion in 2024 to this line item, roughly 9% of revenue. This investment made sense in the era where investors looked towards the future with rose-tinted glasses and rewarded the promise of AI to generate cost efficiencies in cyber, fraud, risk and governance. JPM saw its market cap rise roughly 40% through 2024, catapulting the firm to its highest ever level, as enthusiasm built on the back of the 47th President's pending bonfire of banking regulation and the promise of a more pro-growth and inherently more pro-business policy platform. The reality since then has been somber, with JPM shedding 24% of its market cap from its 52-week peak as the US enters a new reality following “Liberation Day”.

JPM now sees a US recession in 2025 and has raised the odds of this to 60%. This has the potential to usher in a new zeitgeist for JPM when aggregated with a possible rise in CPI and core PCE that will see banking stock valuations crushed. Stagflation defines a rare economic phenomenon where typically parallel economic realities of recession and inflation intersect. In this, the destruction of capital is compounded because the Fed effectively becomes paralyzed. The Fed funds rate can't be cut to spur economic demand because of the rising CPI, and the rate also cannot be hiked because there is a recession. The risk of recession is real with the Atlanta Fed's GDPNow model's latest estimate for 2025 first quarter GDP growth to be negative at 2.8%, down from expectations of a positive economic growth of north of 2% in January.

The Recession Risk, Inflation, Trade Wars, And The Preferreds

JPM generated fiscal 2024 fourth quarter revenue of $43.74 billion, up 13.4% over its year-ago comp and a beat by $2.18 billion on consensus estimates. The company generated net income of $14 billion, up from $9.3 billion a year ago, with EPS of $4.81 beating consensus by a remarkable $0.71 per share and growing by 58% from $3.04 in the year-ago period. Book value per share was up 11% year-over-year to $116.07 with a $2.6 billion provision for credit loss. It was a stellar quarter, but its repetition against the coming economic zeitgeist is in doubt. In the short term, JPM will see its valuation compress with a decline in financial performance set to play out in the medium term. A recession is not good for business and a rise in provision for credit losses should be expected in tow with a decline in loan volume, deposits, and investment banking fees generated.

JPM currently trades on a price-to-sales multiple of 3.6x, around 40% higher than its sector median and beyond its 10-year trend line. The uncertainty now is whether or not the base case for a recession rings true and how long this will last if paired with rising consumer inflation. There is going to be downstream inflation, with China hit with a 104% tariff rate and with tariffs ranging from a 10% minimum to 49% also in place for nearly every single country on Earth. There are also pending tariffs on pharmaceuticals set to come in. We are entering the age of tariffs, a sharp break from the established post-WW2 economic consensus. This is being done at a breakneck pace, with the economic repercussions set to be material and unprecedented. The best-case scenario is a moderate rise in inflation and range-bound GDP growth; the worst-case scenario is a deep recession and a sustained rise in inflation beyond the Fed's point of comfort.

Hence, expectations according to the CME FedWatch Tool that the Fed will cut rates by roughly 100 basis points to roughly 3.25% to 3.50% to end the year seem misplaced. This is being priced in due to recession risk, but a possible rise in inflation will force the Fed to likely hold off on near-term rate cuts. Against this scenario, JPM's fixed-income securities would offer some respite for what's set to be a further selloff of its common shares.

JPMorgan Chase & Co. 5.75% Series DD Preferreds (NYSE:JPM.PR.D) currently offers a 5.9% yield on cost on the back of a $1.44 per share annual coupon payment and preferreds changing hands for $24.30 per share. JPM common shares offer a 2.61% dividend yield. I foresee a scenario where dividends form the core driver of what could be negative total returns in the short to medium term; hence, the preferreds should be weighed here as a trade war between the US and China looks set to upend the US economy.

data.title: Nvidia: 2025 Should Be Strong, But Demand Could Drop In The Years After
data.description:
Investment Thesis

NVIDIA Corporation (NASDAQ:NVDA) has been the hottest stock on Wall Street since ChatGPT’s public release in November 2022. Since then, AI has been at the center stage for most major companies. Either they are making their own AI models, or they are looking for ways to integrate AI into their business. Companies have been investing first, while looking for returns later. Nvidia has been the largest winner in the AI space because of their advanced hardware and, most importantly, their CUDA software.

This article covers the growth potential of Blackwell from a technical and financial perspective. But more importantly, I am going to look at the problems I see ahead for Nvidia. The hyperscalers and alternative cloud providers are critical customers for Nvidia, and cracks are appearing in their business model. Finally, I will cover tariffs and what conditions I am looking for to buy Nvidia given the current volatility.

Nvidia’s Extreme Growth

Nvidia’s revenue and margins have grown rapidly because of growth in their data center segment. AI investment began to rapidly increase in 2023. This has led to Nvidia’s data center revenue increasing 6.67X in just two years, from $15B in 2022 (FY23) to $115B in 2024 (FY25).

Data centers are now Nvidia’s primary source of revenue, generating 88.3% of revenue in FY25. Their gaming and professional visualization segments have seen little growth, floating between $10B to $15B a year. The newer automotive segment is also experiencing high growth but only contributed $1.7B in revenue in FY25. I expect the automotive segment will grow substantially in the coming years as their self-driving car platform advances. Additionally, robotics revenue could also become a major contributor.

The strong demand for AI chips has allowed Nvidia to increase their margins. Before AI took off, their margins were in the mid-40% range, but they have increased to 67% in 2024. I doubt Nvidia will be able to increase margins further because higher prices could weaken demand and incentivize customers to use competing chips. Additionally, higher prices from Nvidia will worsen the profitability of hyperscalers and alternative cloud providers; more on this later.

Blackwell, Nvidia’s Most Demanded GPU Yet

Blackwell (GB200) is Nvidia’s next data center GPU, and it will be the most powerful and likely best-selling entry yet. The GB200 started shipping early this year and will continue to ramp through 2025. During Nvidia’s GTC presentation, Jensen shared that 1.8M GB200s are expected to be shipped to the top four hyperscalers this year. Hopper (H100 & H200) peaked at 1.3M units sold in a single year to the top hyperscalers. It is important to note that each GB200 contains two GPUs packaged as a single unit, therefore the 3.6M GPUs from their presentation are 1.8M units.

The GB200 NVL72 rack (36 Grace CPUs and 72 GB200s with NVLink) is expected to cost $3M each. While standalone GB200 + Grace superchips are expected to cost around $70,000. Assuming hyperscalers only purchase racks since that is the most cost and space-efficient, Nvidia could earn $75B in 2025 revenue from just their top four customers. Large customers likely get volume discounts, but even then, Nvidia will generate substantial revenue from them.

Nvidia also unveiled the Blackwell Ultra (GB300) which provides 50% more performance than the GB200. The processor will start shipping in the second half of 2025.

A key driver for Blackwell's demand is its substantial performance improvement over Hopper. Nvidia benchmarks show the GB200 being 25X to 40X more performance than the H100. The actual performance depends on the data type and is shown in the graph below.

Greater processing throughput allows for more tokens to be generated by a single processor. This will be especially important as reasoning models become more widely used. In the initial wave of AI models, the system would directly answer the prompt. In a reasoning system, the AI automatically re-prompts itself to mimic human reasoning. The more the model thinks, the more tokens it will generate, leading to more computation demand.

Nvidia is betting that greater token production capabilities will lead to more complicated models. At GTC they also teased the performance capabilities of their next GPU, Rubin, which has 13X the performance of Blackwell (see slide 38). Nvidia needs AI model complexity to increase in line with hardware performance improvements. Otherwise, they could end up cannibalizing their future revenues. This would be good for customers but bad for Nvidia.

Decline in GPU Rental Rates

During Jensen’s GTC keynote, he stated that “when Blackwell starts shipping in volume you couldn’t give Hoppers away.” He tried to walk the statement back, but he is 100% correct given the performance improvements. This leads to the first major problem I see Nvidia facing in the future.

Nvidia’s data center customers can be divided into three groups. Hyperscalers are the large cloud providers, including Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) and Microsoft Corporation (MSFT). Then there are alternative cloud providers that are mostly AI-focused, such as CoreWeave, Inc. (CRWV). Finally, there are direct customers who purchase the GPUs for their own use, such as Tesla, Inc. (TSLA). It is especially important to look at hyperscalers and alternative cloud providers because they are speculatively buying Nvidia’s GPUs to rent to AI developers. Disruptions in the rental market will eventually impact Nvidia, and the first cracks are starting to appear.

When demand for H100 chips first began to take off, one of Nvidia’s sales tactics was promoting the high ROI cloud providers could earn. At the time, an H100 could be rented for $4/hour or more. For every dollar invested in Nvidia’s hardware, the cloud provider could earn $5 in rent over 4 years. In 2023, this looked like a great investment. Demand for AI chips had outstripped supply and demand looked like it could only go up. This led to the large investment in AI data center build outs by hyperscalers and alternative cloud providers seen in Nvidia’s large revenue growth.

However, unrelenting demand and supply constraints have come to an end. GPU rental rates have been dropping for some time now, and only hyperscalers have been able to keep their margins high. AWS and Azure still charge around $8/hour for H100s, but alternative cloud providers have had to cut their rates. H100s can be rented for under $2/hour now with most alternative cloud providers charging less than $4/hour.

An excellent data center cost breakdown by Latent Space shows just how precarious a position the alternative cloud providers are in. When looking at the total cost of ownership (infrastructure, GPUs, CPUs, switches, electricity, etc.) the 5-year breakeven price for an H100 is $1.65/hour. To earn a 10% annual return, rental prices must exceed $2.85/hour. These rental rates also assume 100% utilization. Longer idle times waiting for a customer to use the chip increase the required rental rates.

The chart below provides the rates of return and payback times that can be expected for various rental rates and electricity prices. If the rental rate continues to drop it will be unlikely that newly purchased H100s will ever be profitable.

Additionally, Blackwell will likely drive down H100 rental prices due to the substantial performance improvements it provides. Looking at CoreWeave’s pricing, a H100 rents for $6.16/hour while a GB200 rents for $21/hour. For equal performance, a GB200 provides 7.33X more tokens per dollar; assuming the GB200 produces 25X as many tokens as a H100.

As Blackwell becomes more available it’s a no-brainer which chip a company developing or deploying its AI should use. This could result in substantial idle H100 inventory and significant losses for cloud providers that were unable to recoup their investment. Many of the alternative cloud providers are venture capital backed. Though VC funds seem to love throwing money at loss-making companies, it is only a matter of time before the money dries up. This could result in bankruptcies and fewer sales of Nvidia’s future hardware since there are fewer customers to buy their chips. The decreasing rental rates also show a decreased appetite for AI capacity relative to supply. This means Nvidia could be forced to cut their prices to find new willing buyers.

Pull Forward of Demand Due to Large Data Center Build Out

Another concern I have about investing in Nvidia is the probability of a significant pull forward in revenue. If my pull-forward worry is correct, then Nvidia’s current revenue is higher than what it will be long term. I don’t believe Nvidia will go back to pre-AI levels, but I also don’t believe they will be able to maintain current revenues forever. Nvidia should be able to keep revenue high this year, but within the next few years I expect a peak in revenue will be clearly visible.

Nvidia’s revenue increase has mirrored growth in data centers. CBRE Group, Inc. (CBRE) research into data center development expects deliveries in MW capacity to peak in 2024. There have been large data center plans announced since the report was produced, but there have also been cancellations.

The Stargate Project between OpenAI, SoftBank Group Corp. (OTCPK:SFTBY), Oracle Corporation (ORCL), and the UAE MGX investment fund plans to invest $500B over the next four years in building AI data centers. At the same time, Microsoft has backed out of 2GW of planned data center additions. Microsoft is likely pulling back their build out because they are allowing OpenAI to use other providers besides Azure. Stargate will likely backfill the void left by Microsoft. This brings up another concern about new data center developments. Are the large data center announcements adding to previously forecast growth or is it merely a change in who is writing the check.

Improving Efficiency

The decline in new data center capacity could be offset by greater chip efficiency. Nvidia has made great strides in the amount of processing per MW. If efficiency gains are as great as Nvidia projects, then we could be at peak data center build out, but that doesn't mean we are at peak chip demand. Instead, more servers could be located at existing data centers without the need for more power.

I am a structural engineer, and some of the projects I work on are switching stations (small data centers) for a major telecom company. Many of our projects have involved reworking their existing data centers to convert office space into data halls. Since the servers they use have become more power efficient, they can have more servers at the same site without bringing in more power. This saves them significant time and money by not having to build new data centers.

The clash between improved performance and excessive data center build out will leave someone holding the bag. Either speculative data center developers providing space no one requires, or cloud providers fully fitting out data centers only to find insufficient customer demand and lots of idle servers.

I am not alone in this observation. Alibaba Group Holding Limited’s (BABA) chairman called the large AI build out a bubble, with investors “investing ahead of the demand that they’re seeing today.”

AI growth has been pitched as an exponential demand, but exponential demand never lasts. In a similar fashion to Moore’s Law, Huang’s Law states that the performance of GPUs will more than double every two years. Nvidia is doing a wonderful job beating this performance growth rate. In a world where GPU performance grows exponentially, there is no reason to expect data center demand will also grow exponentially.

Impact of Tariffs

The preceding analysis didn’t account for tariffs. Semiconductors are currently exempt, but President Trump has said they are coming soon.

As a reminder, tariffs are paid on the value of the imported product, not the sales price.

Nvidia uses Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM) to produce their chips in Taiwan, but the actual supply chain is even more complicated. Nvidia doesn’t usually sell their GPUs directly to the end customer. Instead, they sell GPUs to an OEM that assembles the chips into servers.

A large portion of the server assembly capacity is in Southeast Asian countries. For now, it is safe to assume that semiconductor tariffs will be similar to the recently enacted tariffs on other goods. The enacted tariffs vary from 24% in Malaysia to 46% in Vietnam.

Hopefully, semiconductors will remain exempt from tariffs due to the large amount already being invested to create new fabs within the US, but this seems unlikely given Trump’s comments. If tariffs are enacted, customers will have to pay more for new servers or OEMs will have to accept lower profit margins. In either case, I expect pressure will be applied to Nvidia to cut prices to help offset any tariff.

Tariffs have come on and off quite rapidly, but I believe this round will be stickier. Investors should closely monitor any future semiconductor tariff announcements because they will hurt Nvidia’s revenue. Either through fewer units sold or lower profit margins. Based on Trump’s reaction to the stock market meltdown, I am assuming tariffs will remain, but the tariff rate might be lowered.

Is Nvidia Worth Investing In

I believe the initial scarcity of AI compute was the primary reason the AI bubble was able to take off. If chips had been more readily available, I doubt the frantic investment in everything AI would have happened. Big data, machine learning, and AI are the same thing, only the marketing spin is different.

What matters most is determining where Nvidia’s revenues are headed because that will determine long-term value. Hype can boost P/E multiples, while fundamentals determine what happens when the hype wears off.

Blackwell should be a major revenue source for Nvidia. If orders don’t get canceled due to tariffs, 2025 should be Nvidia’s highest revenue year yet. Blackwell has a higher price than Hopper, and Blackwell is expected to deliver more units this year than Hopper’s peak year. This is a compelling reason to invest in Nvidia. However, it is unwise to believe Nvidia’s growth streak will continue forever.

Nvidia’s future profitability is dependent on continued AI investments. Falling rental prices indicate abundance, not scarcity. If data center providers are having to cut their rates to find customers, I doubt they will be in a rush to purchase even more chips. Nvidia’s backlog is robust today, but I expect it will fall in the future as current orders are delivered into a weakening server market.

The slowing of data center build out will also slow potential revenue growth and may lead to contractions. New data centers require new servers. Existing data centers will eventually have to refresh their servers, but cloud providers will stretch their update cycles to meet customer demand and their own financial needs.

Initially, when I started working on this article, I was going to give Nvidia a buy rating because of strong Blackwell orders. However, tariffs and market turmoil make buying right now inadvisable. I am giving Nvidia a hold rating for now, but I will outline what I am looking for to make Nvidia a buy.

Current Market Volatility & When to Buy

Nvidia’s share price has been declining since DeepSeek's, from Hangzhou DeepSeek Artificial Intelligence Co., Ltd. (DEEPSEEK), DeepSeek-R1 showed how AI models could be made more efficient. Recent market turmoil has only accelerated the sell-off. The current market uncertainty is creating a compelling buying opportunity, and the further Nvidia falls the greater the potential gain. The chart below shows the last 10 years of Nvidia’s trailing twelve-month GAAP P/E. This is the true measure of Nvidia’s performance and eliminates biases in forward estimate based P/Es.

Looking at their P/E history, Nvidia is currently trading at a better value than the pullback in 2022. Only during the rate hiking cycle in 2018 has Nvidia’s P/E fallen lower. 2018 was also a year marked by tariff uncertainty. Since 2018 is rhyming with 2025, I believe it is best to use it as a guide.

Nvidia has a trailing twelve-month diluted EPS of $2.94 per share. Nvidia’s P/E bottomed in January 2019 at around 17.0X, this would imply a price target of $50 per share. Since timing the market is impossible, I am targeting an entry under a 20X P/E, which is $59 per share. Below this threshold, Nvidia becomes a worthwhile investment if the downtrend no longer looks like a falling knife.

Alternatively, if the tariff drama is resolved—truly, not just speculation about a deal—and markets stabilize, then Nvidia instantly becomes a buy, no matter its price. Some market participants are going risk-off, which has been visible since DeepSeek-R1 came out. Others are hanging on every news drop hoping for recovery. What matters is the macro trajectory, not single-day swings. Once the market has redirected upwards Nvidia becomes a buy, but don’t chase rapid rises on hope.

I view Nvidia as a trade. The potential near-term return is great once markets stabilize, and the further Nvidia falls the better upside potential there is. In the long term, I plan to watch data center construction and AI demand. Any slowing in the growth narrative will be bad for Nvidia’s stock.

data.title: Nvidia: Tariffs Have Strengthened My Conviction (Rating Upgrade To Strong Buy)
data.description:
Scott Bessent on re-shoring plans

Having last covered Nvidia Corporation (NASDAQ: NVDA) ()not long ago, the destruction in broad market equity prices since the time of that article has put stocks on an even a bigger sale than they were at the time.

My logic at the time was as follows:

In growth stories like Nvidia, I look at the PEG ratio in one of two ways, I either draw up a modified PEG ratio adding back research and development expense [R&D], figure how much adjusted op income they have per share, and then multiply the per share adjusted op income by the growth rate, capping it at 25 X [this assumes a max long-term growth rate of 25%.

Or I use the traditional method of looking at the forward expected growth rate, using that as the multiplier times the next year's expected GAAP EPS as the multiplicand. Again, I cap this at 25X, so a stock needs to grow earnings at least at a higher clip than 25% expected to earn my upper-end multiplier. Again, with PEG, a ratio of 1 or less is a fair value to a bargain, depending on how low below a 1 ratio it goes.

As Peter Lynch famously stated (emphasis added):

In general, a p/e ratio that's half the growth rate is very positive, and one that's twice the growth rate is very negative. Lynch, Peter. One Up On Wall Street. 1989 (199)

What has the growth rate in earnings been in recent years? (My favorites are those in the 20 to 25 percent range. I'm wary of companies that seem to be growing faster than 25 percent. Those 50 percenters usually are found in hot industries, and you know what that means.) Lynch, Peter. One Up On Wall Street. 1989 (229).

Taking this into consideration, Nvidia stock looks like a steal should growth rates hold up, and the company be less affected by tariffs. Right now, the company is trading at a forward GAAP P/E ratio of 22.61 X and a very low PEG ratio even if buyers of their product cut CAPEX due to price increases in data centers related to tariffs by quite a bit. There's really no alternative to their GPUs at the moment.

The crux of my argument here to upgrade this to a strong buy [not that I'm the only one of that opinion here] is the exact same negative catalyst that has been crashing the market, tariffs. Re-shoring, or bringing manufacturing back to U.S. territory via tariff threats seems to be the modus operandi of action for the Trump administration for now and the highlight of both Trump and Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent's belief systems.

Treasury Secretary Bessent has been making the rounds eloquently explaining the thought process behind the tariffs, the rationale for bringing the US supply chain of many critical manufactured goods back under control, and how they plan to do it. They believe that smart factories, robotics, and AI could be the key to driving down US manufacturing costs, putting them on par with some of the lowest cost of human capital countries [especially when you include tariffs].

Elon Musk and Tesla (TSLA) have [or at least had] figured out how to produce cars in the U.S. with some of the best margins in the world. It is possible, and I'm sure Musk has championed this possibility behind closed doors. You only pick up these efficiencies by having more AI spend, more AI-related supply chain management systems, and more robotics. Nvidia is the obvious necessary component in this whole scenario, and I would argue it is now becoming a protected U.S. National Security asset to the level of a Boeing (BA) or Lockheed Martin (LMT).

I can only imagine the level of support they will receive in upcoming years as Southeast Asia manufacturing comes to them with investment commitments from Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM). Yes, tariffs for building the chips in Taiwan will cause margins to compress for one of those companies or both, but I assume Taiwan will receive some favorability in negotiations which are already being lined up.

Can you tariff the cloud?

When we talk about Nvidia, yes they make a physical product for domestic use or export, and yes, a retaliatory tariff from a foreign country could make their products more expensive and curb demand. I am going to argue that GPUs go into data centers, data centers are accessed through the cloud, and a GPU could be inserted and used in a more favorable tariff country by an adjacent country with less favorable tariffs.

The same goes for nations that have export bans for Nvidia's products. Through a shell of a shell or a JV of a JV, countries with limited access to Nvidia's products will find access to the product whether the U.S. likes it or not. The cloud is a reality and U.S. adversaries could be spinning servers in Cupertino, California for all we know. If the demand for worldwide compute continues to increase, there are few bans, tariffs or tactics that could limit the demand for Nvidia product in my opinion.

The U.S. would be better off focusing on building the best AI systems that they control, rather than wasting time trying to keep the GPUs out of the hands of others. Additionally, whatever retaliatory tariffs come from other countries, check their schedules, I would doubt they would put Draconian tariffs on critical computer chips that they import from U.S. companies, it would be tantamount to developmental suicide.

Year-to-date performance

In my last article on Nvidia, the company had the second-worst year-to-date performance of the Mag 7, but now Apple (AAPL) has come up the rear and butted them out of the way for 2nd place [or second worst]. Regardless, Apple and Tesla do not seem to be anywhere near as good of a deal as Nvidia, let's take a look at the forward P/E ratios:

Nvidia sits 3rd cheapest on the pile for a forward P/E multiple; however, they also have the highest anticipated forward earnings growth rate. As of current, it's either Alphabet (GOOGL) or Nvidia that has the most attractive valuation in my opinion. Alphabet on an absolute basis and Nvidia on a growth rate to multiple basis. Both are at the top of my list of Mag 7 buys in this 2022 recap of the tech bear market.

The balance sheet is still amazing

The lines for Nvidia on the balance sheet are all going in the right direction. EBITDA is way up and to the right, cash and short-term investments are on the same trajectory, and long-term debt is a blip compared to EBITDA at less than 1/10th [companies should be able to leverage up to 3 X EBITDA if needed]. Shares outstanding have been dropping dramatically, this is certainly a company that is both self-financing and believes in itself. I hope management puts some extra cash to work on this downturn.

The patterns look like a fortunate astrology chart. Mars is in Aries. Love it.

Risks and summary

Nvidia has several risks, but at least financial solvency is not one of those issues like it might be for Intel (INTC), for example. China is breathing down Taiwan's neck in the midst of a heated trade war. Taiwan controls the Fabless company's GPU supply, and the U.S.-capable FABs cannot come online soon enough. Every report of a canceled data center lease by Microsoft (MSFT) or another software hyperscaler rattles the market. Those same hyperscalers are also trying to make competing LLM-capable processors.

Regardless of the above, I recommend any who have not watched Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent's recent round of interviews, especially the one with Tucker Carlson that was over an hour, to have a watch. They truly believe U.S. manufacturing can return, even for small electronic goods. He frequently cites AI and robotics as the way to compress costs.

An example of this might be Red Cat (RCAT), a small Drone company that has products that will replace DJI Chinese drones in the U.S. military and is made in the U.S. state of Utah. A small, low-value-added product. The main way they intend to generate positive margins is through AI-related supply chain management with Palantir (PLTR). It will be worth watching the margins of the low-value add manufacturers more than others because if you can figure out a way to build a small electronic device profitably in the U.S., then we are quickly approaching the point at which AI ROI is a proven fact. At that point, GPU demand only accelerates from there.

Strong buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
